message.content: Access Token Manipulation: Parent PID Spoofing involves adversaries spoofing the parent process identifier (PPID) of a new process to evade detection and elevate privileges. Typically, new processes inherit the PPID from their parent process, but adversaries can explicitly assign a PPID using the CreateProcess API call. This technique can bypass defenses that monitor process spawning, such as those blocking processes initiated from Office documents. By spoofing the PPID, adversaries can make a malicious process appear as if it was spawned by a trusted process, such as PowerShell, rather than an Office document. This can be executed using Visual Basic in a malicious document or any code capable of performing Native API calls. Additionally, assigning a PPID from a privileged process (e.g., SYSTEM) can elevate the new process's privileges. Tools like Cobalt Strike and malware such as KONNI and PipeMon have utilized this technique to spawn processes with alternate PPIDs and elevate privileges. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual parent-child process relationships, implementing strict process creation policies, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and block suspicious process behaviors.
